questions.question: What is Entity Framework Core and how does it differ from Dapper?
questions.answer:
Entity Framework Core (EF Core) is a full ORM — it maps C# classes to database tables, tracks changes, generates SQL, and handles migrations. You mostly write LINQ instead of SQL.

Dapper is a micro-ORM — it extends IDbConnection with extension methods (Query<T>, Execute) that map SQL results to C# objects. You write the SQL yourself.

When I use which:

  • EF Core — standard CRUD, complex object graphs (Include/navigation properties), migrations, where the ORM's generated SQL is fine. FolioTrack used EF Core for all entity management.
  • Dapper — performance-critical queries, complex joins the ORM generates poorly, reporting queries, stored procedures. The offline invoice project used Dapper for all its batch processing queries because we had complex multi-table joins that EF Core over-complicated.

They're not mutually exclusive — in WalletVerification I used EF Core for standard CRUD and injected Dapper (via IDbConnection) for the complex balance calculation queries.
followUps: Can you use EF Core and Dapper together?
What is an ORM?
When would you avoid EF Core?

followUpAnswers.Can you use EF Core and Dapper together?:
Yes — and it's a very practical combination. Both can share the same underlying database connection. The cleanest approach is to retrieve the DbConnection from EF Core's DbContext and pass it to Dapper.

public class PortfolioService
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;

    public PortfolioService(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    // EF Core for standard CRUD
    public async Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Portfolios.FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id, ct);

    // Dapper for complex reporting query
    public async Task<IEnumerable<PortfolioSummaryDto>> GetSummaryReportAsync(int userId)
    {
        var conn = _db.Database.GetDbConnection();
        // Dapper uses the same connection/transaction
        return await conn.QueryAsync<PortfolioSummaryDto>(
            @"SELECT p.Id, p.Name, SUM(h.Quantity * h.PurchasePrice) AS TotalCost,
                     COUNT(h.Id) AS HoldingCount
              FROM Portfolios p
              JOIN Holdings h ON h.PortfolioId = p.Id
              WHERE p.UserId = @UserId
              GROUP BY p.Id, p.Name",
            new { UserId = userId });
    }
}

You can even share the same transaction between EF Core and Dapper by passing the open transaction to Dapper:

await using var tx = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync();
// EF Core work
_db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
// Dapper work in same transaction
var conn = _db.Database.GetDbConnection();
await conn.ExecuteAsync("INSERT INTO AuditLog...", param, tx.GetDbTransaction());
await tx.CommitAsync();

Register IDbConnection separately if you want Dapper used outside of DbContext scope:

builder.Services.AddScoped<IDbConnection>(
    sp => new SqliteConnection(connectionString));
followUpAnswers.What is an ORM?:
ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) is a tool that maps between the object-oriented world (C# classes) and the relational world (database tables), eliminating the impedance mismatch between them.

Without an ORM you write raw SQL and manually map columns to properties:

// Without ORM
using var cmd = conn.CreateCommand();
cmd.CommandText = "SELECT Id, Name, CreatedAt FROM Portfolios WHERE UserId = @uid";
cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@uid", userId);
using var reader = await cmd.ExecuteReaderAsync();
var results = new List<Portfolio>();
while (await reader.ReadAsync())
{
    results.Add(new Portfolio
    {
        Id = reader.GetInt32(0),
        Name = reader.GetString(1),
        CreatedAt = reader.GetDateTime(2)
    });
}

With EF Core (full ORM):

// With EF Core
var results = await _db.Portfolios
    .Where(p => p.UserId == userId)
    .ToListAsync();

What a full ORM provides:

  • Mapping — class properties map to table columns automatically
  • Change tracking — detects which properties changed, generates minimal UPDATE SQL
  • Relationships — navigation properties (portfolio.Holdings) map to foreign keys
  • Migrations — schema evolution tracked as code
  • LINQ translation — C# expressions translated to SQL at runtime

The tradeoff: convenience vs. control. Full ORMs hide SQL from you, which can lead to inefficient queries if you don't check what's being generated.
followUpAnswers.When would you avoid EF Core?:
EF Core is excellent for most scenarios, but there are clear cases to reach for raw SQL or Dapper instead:

  • High-performance bulk operations — EF Core's change tracker adds overhead for batch inserts/updates. EF Core 7+ has ExecuteUpdateAsync/ExecuteDeleteAsync for bulk ops, but for truly massive batches (100k+ rows), SqlBulkCopy or the EFCore.BulkExtensions library is orders of magnitude faster.
  • Complex reporting queries — multi-level aggregation with window functions, CTEs, PIVOT, or DB-specific features that LINQ can't express. Write the SQL by hand and use Dapper or FromSqlInterpolated.
  • Read-only projections with many joins — EF Core's SQL generation for complex Include chains can produce inefficient SQL. Sometimes a hand-written JOIN query returning a flat DTO is cleaner.
  • Stored procedure-heavy legacy databases — EF Core can call stored procedures but the ergonomics are awkward. Dapper is simpler here.
  • Database-specific features — full-text search hints, query hints (WITH (NOLOCK)), table-valued functions — EF Core either doesn't support them or requires raw SQL anyway.
// EF Core 7 bulk update — better than load-and-save
await _db.Invoices
    .Where(i => i.Status == "Draft" && i.CreatedAt < cutoff)
    .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s
        .SetProperty(i => i.Status, "Expired")
        .SetProperty(i => i.UpdatedAt, DateTime.UtcNow));

// For truly massive bulk inserts — skip EF Core entirely
using var bulkCopy = new SqlBulkCopy(connectionString);
bulkCopy.DestinationTableName = "Invoices";
await bulkCopy.WriteToServerAsync(dataTable);

questions.tip: EF Core vs Dapper isn't religious — it's contextual. EF Core productivity wins for simple CRUD. Dapper wins for complex queries. Use both in the same project without guilt.

questions.question: What is DbContext and how should it be used?
questions.answer:
DbContext is the main entry point for EF Core. It represents a session with the database — it tracks entity changes, manages transactions, and translates LINQ to SQL.

Lifetime: DbContext is designed to be short-lived (Scoped). Register it as AddDbContext<T> which defaults to Scoped (one instance per HTTP request). Don't make it Singleton — the change tracker accumulates state and it's not thread-safe.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseSqlServer(connectionString));

// AppDbContext
public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public AppDbContext(DbContextOptions<AppDbContext> options)
        : base(options) { }

    public DbSet<Portfolio> Portfolios { get; set; }
    public DbSet<Holding> Holdings { get; set; }

    protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder builder)
    {
        // Fluent API configuration
        builder.Entity<Portfolio>()
            .HasMany(p => p.Holdings)
            .WithOne(h => h.Portfolio)
            .HasForeignKey(h => h.PortfolioId)
            .OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
    }
}

Don't share DbContext across threads. If you need it in a Singleton or BackgroundService, use IServiceScopeFactory to create a scope and resolve a fresh DbContext per operation.
followUps: What is the Unit of Work pattern in EF Core?
How do you use DbContext in a BackgroundService?

followUpAnswers.What is the Unit of Work pattern in EF C:
The Unit of Work (UoW) pattern groups multiple operations into a single transaction — either all succeed or all fail together. It also defers writes until you explicitly commit, reducing round-trips to the database.

EF Core's DbContext IS a Unit of Work — it tracks all changes across multiple entities and flushes them together in a single SaveChanges() call.

// DbContext acting as Unit of Work:
// Multiple entity changes batched into one transaction
public async Task TransferHoldingAsync(int fromPortfolioId, int toPortfolioId, int holdingId)
{
    var holding = await _db.Holdings.FindAsync(holdingId);
    var fromPortfolio = await _db.Portfolios.FindAsync(fromPortfolioId);
    var toPortfolio = await _db.Portfolios.FindAsync(toPortfolioId);

    holding!.PortfolioId = toPortfolioId;   // tracked change 1
    fromPortfolio!.HoldingCount--;           // tracked change 2
    toPortfolio!.HoldingCount++;             // tracked change 3

    // All three changes committed atomically — one transaction
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
}

When you need explicit transaction control (e.g., coordinating with Dapper or calling stored procedures in the same transaction):

await using var transaction = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync();
try
{
    _db.Portfolios.Add(newPortfolio);
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

    // Also run raw SQL in same transaction
    await _db.Database.ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync(
        $"UPDATE PortfolioStats SET Count = Count + 1 WHERE UserId = {userId}");

    await transaction.CommitAsync();
}
catch
{
    await transaction.RollbackAsync();
    throw;
}

Adding a separate IUnitOfWork abstraction on top of DbContext is rarely needed unless you want to hide the EF Core dependency from your domain layer entirely.
followUpAnswers.How do you use DbContext in a Background:
DbContext is Scoped, but BackgroundService is a Singleton — injecting DbContext directly into a BackgroundService causes a captive dependency bug (scoped inside singleton) and EF Core will throw at runtime.

The correct pattern is to inject IServiceScopeFactory and create a new scope (and thus a new DbContext) for each unit of work:

public class InvoiceReminderService : BackgroundService
{
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory;
    private readonly ILogger<InvoiceReminderService> _logger;

    public InvoiceReminderService(
        IServiceScopeFactory scopeFactory,
        ILogger<InvoiceReminderService> logger)
    {
        _scopeFactory = scopeFactory;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            // Create a fresh scope (and DbContext) for each iteration
            await using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateAsyncScope();
            var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();

            try
            {
                var overdueInvoices = await db.Invoices
                    .Where(i => i.DueDate < DateTime.UtcNow && i.Status == "Unpaid")
                    .ToListAsync(stoppingToken);

                foreach (var invoice in overdueInvoices)
                {
                    invoice.ReminderSentAt = DateTime.UtcNow;
                }
                await db.SaveChangesAsync(stoppingToken);
            }
            catch (Exception ex)
            {
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Error processing invoice reminders");
            }

            // Scope disposed here — DbContext disposed cleanly
            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromHours(1), stoppingToken);
        }
    }
}

Key rules: Always await using the scope so it disposes (and DbContext is released). Never store a reference to the DbContext outside the scope. Each loop iteration gets a fresh DbContext with a clean change tracker.

questions.tip: DbContext is NOT thread-safe. Never share one instance across async operations that may run in parallel. Each scope gets its own instance. This is why it must be Scoped, not Singleton.

questions.question: What is the difference between eager loading, lazy loading, and explicit loading?
questions.answer:
These control how EF Core loads related entities (navigation properties):

Eager loading — load related data in the same query using Include(). SQL JOIN is generated. Best performance when you always need the related data.

var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
        .ThenInclude(h => h.Stock)
    .ToListAsync();

Lazy loading — related data is loaded on first access of the navigation property, transparently. Requires proxies (UseLazyLoadingProxies()). The classic N+1 trap — access portfolio.Holdings in a loop = N separate queries.

// DANGER: lazy loading N+1
foreach (var p in portfolios)
{
    var count = p.Holdings.Count;  // fires a query per portfolio!
}

Explicit loading — manually load related data when needed using Entry().Collection().LoadAsync(). Good for conditional loading (only load holdings if user requests them).

await _db.Entry(portfolio)
    .Collection(p => p.Holdings)
    .LoadAsync();

I disable lazy loading by default — it hides N+1 problems until production. Use eager or explicit loading deliberately.
followUps: How do you detect N+1 queries?
What is AsSplitQuery()?

followUpAnswers.How do you detect N+1 queries?:
An N+1 query occurs when you load a list of N entities and then make an additional query for each one — 1 query for the list plus N queries for related data. With 100 portfolios and lazy loading, that's 101 queries instead of 1 or 2.

Detection methods:

  • EF Core logging — enable SQL logging in development and watch the output. Seeing the same parameterized query repeated N times is the telltale sign.
  • MiniProfiler — integrates with ASP.NET Core and shows all SQL queries per request with timing in a toolbar overlay.
  • Application Insights / OpenTelemetry — EF Core emits query telemetry; you can alert on high query counts per request.
// Enable EF Core SQL logging in Program.cs (development)
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options
        .UseSqlite(connectionString)
        .LogTo(Console.WriteLine, LogLevel.Information)
        .EnableSensitiveDataLogging());

// Or use a warning for N+1 patterns:
options.ConfigureWarnings(w =>
    w.Throw(RelationalEventId.MultipleCollectionIncludeWarning));

EF Core itself will log a warning (MultipleCollectionIncludeWarning) when you Include multiple collection navigations — this is a Cartesian explosion risk that also hints at potential N+1 patterns.

Fix: Use Include() for eager loading, or switch to AsSplitQuery() for multiple collections.
followUpAnswers.What is AsSplitQuery()?:
By default, when you Include() multiple collection navigations, EF Core generates a single SQL query with JOINs. This causes a Cartesian explosion — if a portfolio has 10 holdings and each holding has 10 transactions, the JOIN produces 100 rows just to represent 20 entities.

AsSplitQuery() tells EF Core to issue a separate SQL query for each collection Include, then stitch results together in memory:

// Without AsSplitQuery: one giant JOIN (Cartesian explosion risk)
var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
        .ThenInclude(h => h.Transactions)  // holdings x transactions rows!
    .ToListAsync();

// With AsSplitQuery: 3 separate queries, joined in memory
var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .AsSplitQuery()
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
        .ThenInclude(h => h.Transactions)
    .ToListAsync();

Trade-offs:

Single querySplit query
One round-trip to DBMultiple round-trips
Cartesian explosion with large collectionsManageable result set sizes
Atomic — consistent snapshotNot atomic — data can change between queries
Better for small data setsBetter for large/deep Include chains

Configure globally: optionsBuilder.UseQuerySplittingBehavior(QuerySplittingBehavior.SplitQuery)

questions.tip: AsSplitQuery() tells EF Core to issue separate SQL queries per Include instead of one large JOIN. Avoids Cartesian explosion for multiple collection Includes (joining Portfolios→Holdings→Transactions produces millions of rows if each has many).

questions.question: What are EF Core migrations and how do they work?
questions.answer:
Migrations track schema changes as C# code that can be applied to a database. Each migration has an Up() (apply change) and Down() (roll back) method.

Workflow:

# 1. Create a migration after changing your model
dotnet ef migrations add AddPortfolioDescription

# 2. Review the generated migration file in Migrations/
# EF compares current model to the last snapshot and generates the diff

# 3. Apply to database
dotnet ef database update

# 4. Roll back to previous migration
dotnet ef database update PreviousMigrationName

Auto-apply on startup (for SQLite/small apps):

// Program.cs
using var scope = app.Services.CreateScope();
scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>().Database.Migrate();

Production pattern: Generate SQL script (dotnet ef migrations script), review it, run via DBA or CI pipeline. Never auto-apply migrations in production for SQL Server — you lose control over deployment timing.
followUps: How do you squash migrations?
What is the difference between Migrate() and EnsureCreated()?

followUpAnswers.How do you squash migrations?:
Squashing migrations (also called consolidating or resetting) means collapsing many incremental migration files into one baseline migration that represents the full current schema. This is useful when you have dozens of migrations accumulating over time and want a clean slate.

Steps to squash migrations:

  1. Ensure all environments have the latest migration applied.
  2. Delete all existing migration files from the Migrations/ folder.
  3. Delete the __EFMigrationsHistory table rows from any dev/test databases (or drop and recreate them).
  4. Run dotnet ef migrations add InitialSchema — EF Core compares the model to an empty snapshot and generates one complete migration.
  5. Apply: dotnet ef database update.
# Generate a SQL idempotent script from scratch (alternative approach)
dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent -o schema.sql

Important caution for production: You cannot simply delete migrations if production databases still have old migration history entries. The recommended safe approach is:

  • Keep old migrations in a separate archive folder (or a separate git branch).
  • Create a new baseline migration that the EF migration history table is manually seeded with (mark old entries as applied without running them).

For SQLite development databases (like this app), squashing is much simpler — just delete the .db file and regenerate.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Migrate():
Both methods create the database if it doesn't exist, but they work very differently:

EnsureCreated()Migrate()
What it doesCreates schema from current model snapshot directlyApplies any pending migrations in sequence
Migration historyDoes NOT write to __EFMigrationsHistoryTracks applied migrations in __EFMigrationsHistory
Schema evolutionCannot add migrations later — schema is frozenFully supports incremental schema changes
Use caseUnit tests with in-memory or throw-away SQLite DBsAll real applications (dev, staging, production)
// EnsureCreated: fine for tests
public class TestDbFixture
{
    public AppDbContext CreateContext()
    {
        var options = new DbContextOptionsBuilder<AppDbContext>()
            .UseInMemoryDatabase(Guid.NewGuid().ToString())
            .Options;
        var db = new AppDbContext(options);
        db.Database.EnsureCreated();  // OK here — no migration history needed
        return db;
    }
}

// Migrate: correct for real apps
// Program.cs startup
using var scope = app.Services.CreateScope();
scope.ServiceProvider
    .GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>()
    .Database.Migrate();  // safe to call repeatedly — only runs pending migrations

Critical warning: If you use EnsureCreated() on a real database and then try to add a migration, EF Core will see no migration history and attempt to run all migrations from scratch — conflicting with the existing schema. Always use Migrate() for any database you plan to evolve.

questions.tip: EnsureCreated() vs Migrate(): EnsureCreated() creates the schema from the current model snapshot but doesn't track migration history — you can never add migrations to a DB created this way. Always use Migrate() in real applications.

questions.question: What is the Repository pattern with EF Core? Should you use it?
questions.answer:
The Repository pattern wraps data access behind an abstraction (IPortfolioRepository) so the rest of your application doesn't depend on EF Core directly.

public interface IPortfolioRepository
{
    Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task<List<Portfolio>> GetByUserAsync(string userId, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task AddAsync(Portfolio portfolio);
    Task UpdateAsync(Portfolio portfolio);
    Task DeleteAsync(int id);
}

public class EfPortfolioRepository : IPortfolioRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public EfPortfolioRepository(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;
    // ...implementations using _db.Portfolios...
}

The controversy: EF Core's DbContext already IS a Unit of Work and DbSet<T> already IS a repository. Adding another layer can be over-engineering.

When I use it: When I need to switch data sources (EF Core today, Dapper tomorrow for perf), or for unit testing without hitting the DB (mock the repository). When I don't: simple applications where the extra abstraction adds no value.
followUps: What is the Unit of Work pattern?
How do you unit test code that uses EF Core?

followUpAnswers.What is the Unit of Work pattern?:
The Unit of Work (UoW) pattern maintains a list of objects affected by a business transaction and coordinates writing out changes and resolving concurrency problems. It defers all database writes until you call Commit()/SaveChanges().

The pattern has three responsibilities:

  1. Track which objects have been modified (dirty tracking)
  2. Group multiple repository operations into one atomic transaction
  3. Flush all changes to the database in one batch
// Explicit UoW interface (when you want to hide EF Core)
public interface IUnitOfWork : IDisposable
{
    IPortfolioRepository Portfolios { get; }
    IHoldingRepository Holdings { get; }
    Task<int> SaveChangesAsync(CancellationToken ct = default);
}

public class EfUnitOfWork : IUnitOfWork
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;

    public EfUnitOfWork(AppDbContext db)
    {
        _db = db;
        Portfolios = new EfPortfolioRepository(db);
        Holdings = new EfHoldingRepository(db);
    }

    public IPortfolioRepository Portfolios { get; }
    public IHoldingRepository Holdings { get; }
    public Task<int> SaveChangesAsync(CancellationToken ct = default)
        => _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    public void Dispose() => _db.Dispose();
}

// Usage in service layer
public async Task CreatePortfolioWithHoldingAsync(CreatePortfolioRequest req)
{
    var portfolio = new Portfolio { Name = req.Name, UserId = req.UserId };
    await _uow.Portfolios.AddAsync(portfolio);

    var holding = new Holding { Stock = req.Stock, Quantity = req.Quantity };
    await _uow.Holdings.AddAsync(holding);

    await _uow.SaveChangesAsync();  // one transaction for both
}

In most EF Core applications, DbContext directly serves as the UoW. The explicit interface adds value primarily when: (1) you want to test services without any EF Core dependency, or (2) you're mixing multiple data sources in one transaction.
followUpAnswers.How do you unit test code that uses EF C:
There are three main approaches, each with different trade-offs:

1. In-memory SQLite (recommended for most tests) — real SQLite engine, no mocking, supports migrations:

public class PortfolioServiceTests
{
    private AppDbContext CreateDb()
    {
        var options = new DbContextOptionsBuilder<AppDbContext>()
            .UseSqlite("DataSource=:memory:")
            .Options;
        var db = new AppDbContext(options);
        db.Database.OpenConnection();   // keep connection open
        db.Database.EnsureCreated();
        return db;
    }

    [Fact]
    public async Task GetByUser_ReturnsOnlyUsersPortfolios()
    {
        await using var db = CreateDb();
        db.Portfolios.AddRange(
            new Portfolio { UserId = "user1", Name = "P1" },
            new Portfolio { UserId = "user2", Name = "P2" });
        await db.SaveChangesAsync();

        var svc = new PortfolioService(db);
        var results = await svc.GetByUserAsync("user1");

        Assert.Single(results);
        Assert.Equal("P1", results[0].Name);
    }
}

2. EF Core InMemory provider — simplest setup but has quirks (no FK constraints, no transactions, no raw SQL):

var options = new DbContextOptionsBuilder<AppDbContext>()
    .UseInMemoryDatabase(databaseName: Guid.NewGuid().ToString())
    .Options;

3. Mock the Repository interface — fastest unit tests, no DB at all. Best when testing pure business logic:

var mockRepo = new Mock<IPortfolioRepository>();
mockRepo.Setup(r => r.GetByUserAsync("user1", default))
    .ReturnsAsync(new List<Portfolio> { new() { Name = "P1" } });

var svc = new PortfolioService(mockRepo.Object);
var results = await svc.GetByUserAsync("user1");
// Assert business logic outcomes

My preference: SQLite in-memory for integration-style service tests, mocked repositories only when the test is purely about business logic with no data access concern.

questions.tip: EF Core InMemory or SQLite in-memory lets you test without mocking the repository at all. This is often simpler than setting up mocks. Only use repository mocks when you specifically want to test business logic in isolation from all data access.

questions.question: What is AsNoTracking() and when should you use it?
questions.answer:
By default, EF Core tracks every entity it loads — the change tracker stores the original values so it can detect what changed for SaveChanges(). AsNoTracking() disables this.

When to use AsNoTracking():

  • Read-only queries (API GET endpoints that return data without modifying it)
  • Reporting/dashboard queries loading many rows
  • Any query where you won't call SaveChanges() on the loaded entities
// READ-ONLY: no tracking needed, faster and less memory
var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .AsNoTracking()
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
    .Where(p => p.UserId == userId)
    .ToListAsync();

// READ-WRITE: tracking needed
var portfolio = await _db.Portfolios
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id);  // tracked
portfolio!.Name = "New Name";
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // change detector knows Name changed

Performance impact: AsNoTracking() is measurably faster for large result sets — no identity map lookups, no snapshot storage. In FolioTrack's dashboard query (200+ portfolio items), it cut query time by ~30%.
followUps: What is AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResolution()?
What happens if you modify an AsNoTracking entity and SaveChanges?

followUpAnswers.What is AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResoluti:
AsNoTracking() has a subtle problem when your query returns related entities that appear more than once — it creates duplicate object instances instead of reusing the same reference. AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResolution() fixes this.

The problem:

// Suppose Stock 'AAPL' appears in multiple holdings
var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .AsNoTracking()
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
        .ThenInclude(h => h.Stock)
    .ToListAsync();

// With AsNoTracking: AAPL appears as TWO separate Stock objects
// portfolios[0].Holdings[0].Stock != portfolios[0].Holdings[1].Stock
// (even though both represent AAPL — same Id, different references)
var stock1 = portfolios[0].Holdings[0].Stock;
var stock2 = portfolios[0].Holdings[1].Stock;
bool sameRef = ReferenceEquals(stock1, stock2);  // FALSE with AsNoTracking!

AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResolution() maintains an identity map (like full tracking) but still doesn't track changes — you get object identity without the write-tracking overhead:

var portfolios = await _db.Portfolios
    .AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResolution()  // identity map, no change tracking
    .Include(p => p.Holdings)
        .ThenInclude(h => h.Stock)
    .ToListAsync();

// Now AAPL Stock is the same object reference everywhere
bool sameRef = ReferenceEquals(
    portfolios[0].Holdings[0].Stock,
    portfolios[0].Holdings[1].Stock);  // TRUE

When to use it: When your query returns entities with shared related objects (many holdings referencing the same stock, many orders for the same customer) and you care about reference equality — for example, if you're doing in-memory grouping or deduplication by reference.
followUpAnswers.What happens if you modify an AsNoTracki:
The short answer: nothing happens — EF Core silently ignores the modification because the entity is not in the change tracker.

// Load with no tracking
var portfolio = await _db.Portfolios
    .AsNoTracking()
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id);

// Modify the object in memory
portfolio!.Name = "New Name";  // modifies the C# object only

// SaveChanges — EF Core has no knowledge of this entity
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // NOTHING is saved! No UPDATE generated.

The entity is not in the change tracker, so EF Core generates zero SQL for it. This is a silent failure — no exception, no warning, just no database write.

If you need to update an AsNoTracking entity, you have two options:

// Option 1: Attach and mark Modified (generates UPDATE for all columns)
_db.Attach(portfolio);
_db.Entry(portfolio).State = EntityState.Modified;
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

// Option 2: Use Update() (same effect — marks entire entity Modified)
_db.Update(portfolio);
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

// Option 3 (best): Reload tracked, modify, save (only changed columns updated)
var tracked = await _db.Portfolios.FindAsync(portfolio.Id);
tracked!.Name = portfolio.Name;  // apply changes
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // minimal UPDATE

Option 1/2 generate an UPDATE for all columns (even unchanged ones), which can overwrite concurrent changes. Option 3 is safer and generates a minimal UPDATE statement.

questions.tip: Configure AsNoTracking globally for read-heavy apps: optionsBuilder.UseQueryTrackingBehavior(QueryTrackingBehavior.NoTracking) in OnConfiguring, then use AsTracking() only for the write operations. Flips the default.

questions.question: How do you handle concurrency conflicts in EF Core?
questions.answer:
EF Core supports optimistic concurrency via rowversion (timestamp) columns. When you update an entity, EF Core adds a WHERE RowVersion = @originalVersion clause. If another request modified the row since you read it, 0 rows are affected and EF Core throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException.

// Model
public class Wallet
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public decimal Balance { get; set; }

    [Timestamp]  // maps to SQL rowversion column
    public byte[] RowVersion { get; set; } = [];
}

// Service
public async Task DeductBalanceAsync(int walletId, decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
{
    var wallet = await _db.Wallets.FindAsync([walletId], ct);
    wallet!.Balance -= amount;

    try
    {
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    }
    catch (DbUpdateConcurrencyException ex)
    {
        // Reload and retry or throw a domain exception
        var entry = ex.Entries.Single();
        await entry.ReloadAsync(ct);  // reload with current DB values
        throw new BusinessException("Concurrent modification — please retry.");
    }
}

projectExample.explanation: WalletVerification used timestamp-based optimistic concurrency on the Wallet entity to prevent double top-up from rapid button taps.

followUps: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency?
How do you do pessimistic locking with EF Core?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between optimisti:
Both strategies prevent lost updates (two users overwriting each other's changes), but they differ in when they check for conflicts:

OptimisticPessimistic
AssumptionConflicts are rare — let them happen, detect at saveConflicts are likely — prevent them upfront
MechanismRowversion / concurrency token checked at UPDATEDatabase row lock held from read until commit
Conflict handlingDbUpdateConcurrencyException thrown — retry or mergeOther transactions block and wait (or timeout)
ThroughputHigh — no blocking between readers and writersLower — contention under high load
Deadlock riskNoneYes — if lock order isn't consistent
Best forWeb apps (short-lived requests, rare contention)Financial systems, inventory where contention is expected

Optimistic concurrency is almost always preferred for web applications. EF Core supports it natively via [Timestamp] or [ConcurrencyCheck]:

// [ConcurrencyCheck] on any property — EF includes it in WHERE clause
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [ConcurrencyCheck]
    public decimal Price { get; set; }  // if Price changed since we read it, throw
}
followUpAnswers.How do you do pessimistic locking with E:
EF Core has no built-in API for pessimistic locking — you must drop down to raw SQL with database-specific lock hints. This is by design: locks are inherently database-specific.

SQL Server: UPDLOCK + ROWLOCK

public async Task<Wallet> GetWalletForUpdateAsync(int walletId, CancellationToken ct)
{
    // Must be inside a transaction for the lock to be meaningful
    await using var tx = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(ct);

    // Acquire update lock — other transactions block on this row
    var wallet = await _db.Wallets
        .FromSqlInterpolated(
            $"SELECT * FROM Wallets WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK) WHERE Id = {walletId}")
        .FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);

    // Do work...
    wallet!.Balance -= amount;
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    await tx.CommitAsync(ct);  // lock released on commit
    return wallet!;
}

PostgreSQL: FOR UPDATE

var wallet = await _db.Wallets
    .FromSqlInterpolated(
        $"SELECT * FROM \"Wallets\" WHERE \"Id\" = {walletId} FOR UPDATE")
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);

SQLite: No row-level locks — uses database-level write locks. Not suitable for high-concurrency pessimistic scenarios.

Practical advice: Pessimistic locking is a last resort. Try optimistic concurrency first. If contention is genuinely high (e.g., shared inventory counter), consider using database sequences, atomic UPDATE ... SET balance = balance - @amount WHERE balance >= @amount, or a dedicated queue/saga pattern instead of row locks.

questions.tip: Pessimistic locking with EF Core: Use raw SQL with WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK) hint inside a transaction: _db.Database.ExecuteSqlRawAsync("SELECT... WITH (UPDLOCK)"). EF Core has no built-in API for lock hints — you have to drop down to raw SQL.

questions.question: How do you use raw SQL in EF Core?
questions.answer:
Sometimes EF Core's LINQ translator doesn't generate optimal SQL, or you need DB-specific features. EF Core provides safe ways to drop to raw SQL:

// 1. FromSqlInterpolated: for queries returning entities
//    Uses parameterized SQL (safe from injection)
var invoices = await _db.Invoices
    .FromSqlInterpolated(
        $"SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = {clientId} AND Year = {year}")
    .Include(i => i.LineItems)  // can still chain LINQ!
    .ToListAsync();

// 2. ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync: for non-query commands
int rows = await _db.Database.ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync(
    $"UPDATE Invoices SET Status = 'Archived' WHERE DueDate < {cutoffDate}");

// 3. Dapper alongside EF Core: for complex reporting queries
var connection = _db.Database.GetDbConnection();
var report = await connection.QueryAsync<InvoiceSummaryDto>(
    "EXEC sp_InvoiceSummary @year", new { year = 2024 });

NEVER use FromSqlRaw with string concatenation — it's injection-vulnerable. Always use FromSqlInterpolated or explicit SqlParameter objects with FromSqlRaw.
followUps: What is SqlParameter vs interpolated strings?
How do you call a stored procedure with EF Core?

followUpAnswers.What is SqlParameter vs interpolated str:
Both protect against SQL injection, but they work differently and have different use cases:

FromSqlInterpolated / interpolated strings: EF Core intercepts the C# FormattableString before it's converted to a string — the values are extracted and passed as SqlParameter objects automatically. The SQL string itself never contains the values.

// Safe — EF Core converts {clientId} to @p0 parameter
var results = await _db.Invoices
    .FromSqlInterpolated($"SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = {clientId}")
    .ToListAsync();
// Generated: SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @p0
//            with SqlParameter("@p0", clientId)

SqlParameter with FromSqlRaw: You create the parameters explicitly. More verbose but useful when building dynamic SQL with conditional parameters:

// Explicit SqlParameter — safe and useful for dynamic queries
var clientParam = new SqlParameter("@clientId", clientId);
var yearParam = new SqlParameter("@year", year);

var results = await _db.Invoices
    .FromSqlRaw(
        "SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = @clientId AND Year = @year",
        clientParam, yearParam)
    .ToListAsync();

NEVER do this — SQL injection vulnerability:

// DANGEROUS: direct string interpolation bypasses parameterization
var sql = $"SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE ClientId = {clientId}";  // injection!
var results = await _db.Invoices.FromSqlRaw(sql).ToListAsync();

The key distinction: $"..." passed to FromSqlInterpolated is safe (EF intercepts). The same $"..." built as a plain string first and then passed to FromSqlRaw is unsafe — it's already been interpolated into a string before EF sees it.
followUpAnswers.How do you call a stored procedure with:
EF Core supports stored procedures via raw SQL methods. The approach depends on whether the procedure returns entities or scalar/non-entity results:

Returning entities (FromSqlInterpolated):

// Stored proc returning Invoice rows — maps to entity
var invoices = await _db.Invoices
    .FromSqlInterpolated($"EXEC sp_GetInvoicesByClient @ClientId = {clientId}")
    .AsNoTracking()
    .ToListAsync();

Returning non-entity results — use a keyless entity:

// 1. Define a keyless result type
[Keyless]
public class InvoiceSummaryResult
{
    public int ClientId { get; set; }
    public decimal TotalAmount { get; set; }
    public int InvoiceCount { get; set; }
}

// 2. Register in DbContext
public DbSet<InvoiceSummaryResult> InvoiceSummaries => Set<InvoiceSummaryResult>();

// 3. Call the proc
var summary = await _db.InvoiceSummaries
    .FromSqlInterpolated($"EXEC sp_InvoiceSummary @Year = {year}")
    .ToListAsync();

Non-query stored procedures (ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync):

// Proc that doesn't return rows (archive, cleanup, etc.)
await _db.Database.ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync(
    $"EXEC sp_ArchiveOldInvoices @CutoffDate = {cutoffDate}, @BatchSize = {batchSize}");

Using Dapper for complex stored procedures (often simpler):

var conn = _db.Database.GetDbConnection();
var results = await conn.QueryAsync<InvoiceSummaryDto>(
    "sp_InvoiceSummary",
    new { Year = year, ClientId = clientId },
    commandType: CommandType.StoredProcedure);

questions.tip: FromSqlInterpolated is NOT the same as $"..." string interpolation. EF Core intercepts the FormattableString and converts {clientId} into a parameterized @p0 — the value is never concatenated into the SQL string. This is safe from injection.

questions.question: What is the difference between Add(), Attach(), and Update() in EF Core?
questions.answer:
These methods set the entity state in EF Core's change tracker:

  • Add(entity) — marks as EntityState.Added. SaveChanges generates an INSERT. For new entities the DB hasn't seen.
  • Attach(entity) — marks as EntityState.Unchanged. No SQL generated unless you then modify properties. Use when you have a disconnected entity (e.g., from a DTO) that you know exists in DB but was loaded outside this DbContext.
  • Update(entity) — marks as EntityState.Modified. SaveChanges generates an UPDATE for all columns (not just changed ones). For disconnected scenarios where you want to overwrite everything.
// Disconnected update pattern (common in APIs)
[HttpPut("{id}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Update(int id, UpdatePortfolioRequest req)
{
    var exists = await _db.Portfolios.AnyAsync(p => p.Id == id);
    if (!exists) return NotFound();

    var entity = new Portfolio { Id = id, Name = req.Name, ... };
    _db.Update(entity);  // marks Modified, generates UPDATE all columns
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    return NoContent();
}

// Better: load, modify, save (only changed columns updated)
var entity = await _db.Portfolios.FindAsync(id);
entity!.Name = req.Name;  // only Name is Modified
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // UPDATE Portfolios SET Name = ... WHERE Id = ...
followUps: What are all EF Core EntityState values?
What is ExecuteUpdateAsync (EF Core 7)?

followUpAnswers.What are all EF Core EntityState values?:
EF Core's change tracker assigns each entity one of five states:

StateDescriptionSaveChanges action
DetachedEntity is not tracked by the DbContext at allNothing — not known to the tracker
UnchangedEntity is tracked; no properties have changed since it was loaded or attachedNothing — no SQL generated
AddedEntity is new; tracked by context but doesn't exist in DB yetINSERT statement generated
ModifiedEntity exists in DB and one or more properties have been changedUPDATE statement generated (all or dirty columns)
DeletedEntity is tracked and marked for removal from DBDELETE statement generated
var portfolio = await _db.Portfolios.FindAsync(id);
Console.WriteLine(_db.Entry(portfolio!).State);  // Unchanged

portfolio!.Name = "New Name";
Console.WriteLine(_db.Entry(portfolio).State);  // Modified

_db.Portfolios.Remove(portfolio);
Console.WriteLine(_db.Entry(portfolio).State);  // Deleted

var newPortfolio = new Portfolio { Name = "Brand New" };
_db.Portfolios.Add(newPortfolio);
Console.WriteLine(_db.Entry(newPortfolio).State);  // Added

// Manually set state
_db.Entry(portfolio).State = EntityState.Detached;  // stop tracking

You can inspect all tracked entities: _db.ChangeTracker.Entries().Where(e => e.State == EntityState.Modified)
followUpAnswers.What is ExecuteUpdateAsync (EF Core 7)?:
ExecuteUpdateAsync (introduced in EF Core 7) performs a bulk UPDATE directly in the database without loading entities into memory. It translates a LINQ query + property setters into a single SQL UPDATE statement.

// EF Core 7+: bulk update without loading entities
// Before: load all, modify each, SaveChanges (N entities loaded into memory)
var invoices = await _db.Invoices
    .Where(i => i.Status == "Draft" && i.CreatedAt < cutoff)
    .ToListAsync();
foreach (var i in invoices) { i.Status = "Expired"; }
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();  // N individual UPDATEs or one batched UPDATE

// After: single SQL UPDATE, zero entities loaded
await _db.Invoices
    .Where(i => i.Status == "Draft" && i.CreatedAt < cutoff)
    .ExecuteUpdateAsync(setters => setters
        .SetProperty(i => i.Status, "Expired")
        .SetProperty(i => i.UpdatedAt, DateTime.UtcNow));
// SQL: UPDATE Invoices SET Status = 'Expired', UpdatedAt = @now
//       WHERE Status = 'Draft' AND CreatedAt < @cutoff

Similarly, ExecuteDeleteAsync does bulk DELETE:

int deleted = await _db.AuditLogs
    .Where(l => l.CreatedAt < DateTime.UtcNow.AddYears(-2))
    .ExecuteDeleteAsync();
// SQL: DELETE FROM AuditLogs WHERE CreatedAt < @cutoff

Important difference from SaveChanges: ExecuteUpdateAsync and ExecuteDeleteAsync bypass the change tracker entirely. They do not trigger SaveChanges interceptors, do not fire ChangeTracker events, and do not respect concurrency tokens. Use them for bulk operations where those concerns don't apply.

questions.tip: EF Core 7's ExecuteUpdateAsync: _db.Portfolios.Where(p => p.UserId == userId).ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s.SetProperty(p => p.IsActive, false)) — bulk update without loading entities. Much more efficient than load-modify-save for batch operations.

questions.question: How do you configure EF Core using Fluent API vs Data Annotations?
questions.answer:
Both configure the EF Core model, but they differ in placement and capability:

Data Annotations — attributes on the model class. Simple but pollutes domain models with infrastructure concerns.

[Table("Portfolios")]
public class Portfolio
{
    [Key]
    public int Id { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [MaxLength(200)]
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Column(TypeName = "decimal(18,4)")]
    public decimal InitialValue { get; set; }
}

Fluent API — in OnModelCreating. More powerful, doesn't touch domain models (keeps them clean), better for complex configurations.

protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder builder)
{
    builder.Entity<Portfolio>(e =>
    {
        e.ToTable("Portfolios");
        e.HasKey(p => p.Id);
        e.Property(p => p.Name).IsRequired().HasMaxLength(200);
        e.Property(p => p.InitialValue).HasColumnType("decimal(18,4)");
        e.HasIndex(p => p.UserId).HasDatabaseName("IX_Portfolios_UserId");
        e.HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted);  // global soft-delete filter
    });
}

I use Fluent API for all configuration — cleaner separation of infrastructure from domain. I split configuration into IEntityTypeConfiguration<T> classes per entity and register with builder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly().
followUps: What is IEntityTypeConfiguration?
What is a global query filter?

followUpAnswers.What is IEntityTypeConfiguration?:
IEntityTypeConfiguration<TEntity> is an interface that lets you move each entity's Fluent API configuration into its own dedicated class, keeping OnModelCreating clean and each configuration focused and testable.

// Instead of everything in OnModelCreating, create per-entity config classes:
public class PortfolioConfiguration : IEntityTypeConfiguration<Portfolio>
{
    public void Configure(EntityTypeBuilder<Portfolio> builder)
    {
        builder.ToTable("Portfolios");

        builder.HasKey(p => p.Id);

        builder.Property(p => p.Name)
            .IsRequired()
            .HasMaxLength(200);

        builder.Property(p => p.InitialValue)
            .HasColumnType("decimal(18,4)");

        builder.HasIndex(p => p.UserId)
            .HasDatabaseName("IX_Portfolios_UserId");

        builder.HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted);

        builder.HasMany(p => p.Holdings)
            .WithOne(h => h.Portfolio)
            .HasForeignKey(h => h.PortfolioId)
            .OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
    }
}

public class HoldingConfiguration : IEntityTypeConfiguration<Holding>
{
    public void Configure(EntityTypeBuilder<Holding> builder)
    {
        builder.ToTable("Holdings");
        builder.Property(h => h.PurchasePrice).HasColumnType("decimal(18,4)");
        // ...
    }
}

Register all configurations in one line in OnModelCreating:

protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder builder)
{
    // Scans the assembly for all IEntityTypeConfiguration implementations
    builder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly(typeof(AppDbContext).Assembly);
}

This is the pattern I always use — it scales cleanly as the model grows and makes each configuration independently reviewable.
followUpAnswers.What is a global query filter?:
A global query filter is a LINQ predicate added to an entity's model configuration that EF Core automatically appends to every query for that entity. It's applied transparently — you don't have to remember to add .Where() manually on every query.

Two primary use cases:

1. Soft delete — entities are never physically deleted, just marked IsDeleted = true:

// Configuration
builder.Entity<Invoice>()
    .HasQueryFilter(i => !i.IsDeleted);

// Every query automatically excludes soft-deleted rows:
var invoices = await _db.Invoices.ToListAsync();
// SQL: SELECT * FROM Invoices WHERE IsDeleted = 0  <-- filter added automatically

// Override the filter when needed (e.g., admin restore feature)
var deleted = await _db.Invoices
    .IgnoreQueryFilters()
    .Where(i => i.IsDeleted)
    .ToListAsync();

2. Multi-tenancy — each tenant only sees their own data:

public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    private readonly ICurrentTenantService _tenant;

    public AppDbContext(DbContextOptions options, ICurrentTenantService tenant)
        : base(options)
    {
        _tenant = tenant;
    }

    protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder builder)
    {
        // All tenant-scoped entities automatically filtered
        builder.Entity<Portfolio>()
            .HasQueryFilter(p => p.TenantId == _tenant.TenantId);
        builder.Entity<Invoice>()
            .HasQueryFilter(i => i.TenantId == _tenant.TenantId);
    }
}

Important considerations:

  • Filters apply to all queries including Include()-loaded related entities
  • Use .IgnoreQueryFilters() when you legitimately need to bypass them
  • Filters can reference injected services (like ICurrentTenantService) but they must be resolved at query time, not at model build time

questions.tip: Global query filters are powerful for soft delete and multi-tenancy: e.HasQueryFilter(p => p.TenantId == _tenantId && !p.IsDeleted). Every query on that entity automatically includes the filter. Override with .IgnoreQueryFilters() when needed.
